question: What is the time complexity of finding the longest palindromic substring using Dynamic Programming?

problem.title: Longest Palindromic Substring
problem.description: Given a string s, return the longest palindromic substring in s.
constraints: s consists only of digits and English letters
hints: Use expand around center approach for O(n²) time and O(1) space
Or use DP table dp[i][j] = true if s[i..j] is palindrome

explanation: The DP approach uses a 2D table where dp[i][j] = true if s[i..j] is a palindrome. Time: O(n²), Space: O(n²). The expand-around-center approach achieves the same time complexity with O(1) space.

question: For the Two Sum problem, which approach gives O(n) time complexity?

problem.title: Two Sum
problem.description: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target.
problem.solution: Use a hash map to store {value: index}. For each element, check if (target - element) exists in the map. Time: O(n), Space: O(n).

explanation: Hash Map approach: For each element nums[i], check if (target - nums[i]) exists in the hash map. If yes, return [stored_index, i]. If no, store {nums[i]: i}. Single pass = O(n) time. This is the most commonly asked coding question in IT company interviews.

question: What algorithm is most efficient for detecting a cycle in a linked list?
options: Floyd's Cycle Detection Algorithm (Slow & Fast pointers)
correctAnswer: Floyd's Cycle Detection Algorithm (Slow & Fast pointers)

problem.title: Linked List Cycle Detection
problem.description: Given the head of a linked list, determine if the linked list has a cycle. A cycle exists if some node can be reached again by following the next pointers.
constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [0, 10⁴]
problem.solution: Use two pointers: slow (moves 1 step) and fast (moves 2 steps). If they meet, there's a cycle. Time: O(n), Space: O(1).

explanation: Floyd's Cycle Detection (Tortoise and Hare): slow pointer moves 1 step, fast pointer moves 2 steps. If a cycle exists, they will eventually meet. Time: O(n), Space: O(1) — much better than using a HashSet which needs O(n) space.

question: What is the time complexity of a Fibonacci implementation using memoization (Top-Down DP)?

problem.title: Fibonacci Number
problem.description: The Fibonacci numbers are defined as: F(0) = 0, F(1) = 1, F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2). Given n, calculate F(n).
problem.solution:
def fib(n, memo={}):
  if n <= 1: return n
  if n in memo: return memo[n]
  memo[n] = fib(n-1, memo) + fib(n-2, memo)
  return memo[n]

explanation: Naive recursion is O(2^n) as it recomputes subproblems. Memoization stores computed results, so each subproblem is solved only once — making it O(n) time and O(n) space. Bottom-up DP also achieves O(n) time with O(n) or O(1) space.

question: Which traversal visits nodes in the order: Left → Root → Right?

problem.title: Binary Tree In-order Traversal
problem.description: Given the root of a binary tree, return the inorder traversal of its nodes' values.

explanation: In-order traversal (Left → Root → Right) of a Binary Search Tree produces values in sorted ascending order — a key property! Pre-order (Root → Left → Right) is useful for copying a tree. Post-order (Left → Right → Root) is useful for deleting a tree.
